findings.title: Registry ordering creates silent routing dependency
findings.suggested_fix: Add a comment above the Vercel AI SDK entry: '// IMPORTANT: Must come after provider-specific entries. @ai-sdk/* wildcard matches any @ai-sdk/ prefix, so provider-specific patterns (e.g. @ai-sdk/google) must be listed before this entry to route correctly.'
findings.why_it_matters: If a developer reorders registry entries, @ai-sdk/google would match the Vercel SDK entry before Google, silently misattributing data to the wrong processor in the drift-check output. No test covers ordering.
evidence: SUBPROCESSOR_REGISTRY.find() returns the first match; Google entry at line 60 must precede Vercel entry at line 82 for @ai-sdk/google to route to Google

findings.title: No unit tests for registry helpers or script logic
findings.suggested_fix: Add src/lib/compliance/__tests__/subprocessorRegistry.test.ts with: (1) findSubProcessorForPackage happy-path for each known package pattern, (2) ordering test verifying @ai-sdk/google → Google not Vercel, (3) isFirstPartyInfra test for @mlc-ai/web-llm, (4) verify no duplicate names in registry.
findings.why_it_matters: AC-7 specifies the script should exit non-zero on unlisted packages but no tests verify this invariant. The pattern-matching logic in findSubProcessorForPackage has ordering dependencies and wildcard behavior that are regression-prone.
evidence: No test file exists under src/lib/compliance/__tests__/ for subprocessorRegistry.ts
SUBPROCESSOR_REGISTRY.find() ordering dependency between Google (@ai-sdk/google exact) and Vercel (@ai-sdk/* wildcard) is untested

findings.title: Unnecessary cast in isFirstPartyInfra
findings.why_it_matters: The cast (FIRST_PARTY_INFRA as readonly string[]).includes() is redundant — FIRST_PARTY_INFRA is already typed as readonly string[]. The cast adds noise without value.
evidence: Line 155: `return (FIRST_PARTY_INFRA as readonly string[]).includes(packageName)` — FIRST_PARTY_INFRA is declared as `readonly string[]` at line 124

findings.title: DPA URLs marked placeholder without machine-readable status
findings.why_it_matters: Several DPA URL fields contain '[operator to verify current version date]' which is human-readable but not machine-checkable. If the annual-review checklist doesn't explicitly call these out, they may remain unverified. Advisory: ensure annual-review.md (S13) explicitly enumerates the placeholder DPA entries.
evidence: subprocessors.md rows for Cloudflare, Stripe, Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Groq, Sentry, Resend all contain '[operator to verify' markers

residual_risks: The drift-check script only covers npm packages. Env var references to external services (e.g. VITE_SENTRY_DSN, OPENAI_API_KEY) that indicate a service is used are not scanned. Story AC-7 mentioned env var scanning but the implementation only scans package.json.

The Vercel AI SDK entry covers all @ai-sdk/* packages with a wildcard, meaning any new @ai-sdk/<provider> addition would match Vercel (not the underlying provider) in the drift check. This could mask a new AI provider being added without a specific DPA entry.
testing_gaps: No unit test for findSubProcessorForPackage with @ai-sdk/google → Google routing
No unit test for verify-subprocessors.ts exit code behavior
